segments.text: There's a microchip in every NFL football. So, why are these people still measuring where the ball goes with two sticks and a chain? The answer says a
lot about robots versus refs across sports. Every NFL football now has a sensor about the size of a squished pee right here between the air bladder and
the leather skin that blinks 10 times a second, sending radio waves that track the speed and the 2D location of the ball. But refs don't usually use it.
It's mostly used for stats like you see on TV and just for decisions on punts and postgame penalties, but why? Well,
10 times a second sounds like a lot, but the ball is going so fast it can travel over 8 ft between every blink. So, in a
game where a touchdown is decided by a fraction of an inch, an 8 ft blind spot is a massive liability. They could increase that rate, but then the sensors
would get heavier and the ball might feel off. To make things worse, a sensor is just a single point inside the ball.
So, there are times when the sensor doesn't cross the end zone, but the tip might, so it's still a touchdown. But they are using brand new tech this year to make calls more accurate. The
solution isn't inside the ball. It's all around the stadium. The NFL just installed new high-speed cameras that track the ball and 29 specific points on
every player's body at 60 frames per second. This will be the first Super Bowl with this new system. And don't worry, the chain gang is still there to
check. If you want to see more optimistic science and tech stories, subscribe.
